docs.title: Genomics of Neurodegenerative Diseases and Aging Lab
docs.text: This documentation covers lab infrastructure usage, dry-lab workflows, and data management practices.

docs.title: Main sections
docs.text:
• Start here: first login, access model, and quick safety rules.
  • Dry lab: Linux basics, Slurm on Spider, environments, containers, tmux, and Git workflow.
  • Data management: project structure, storage and transfer, GDPR constraints, and good practices.
  • Wet lab: reserved for wet-lab SOPs and sample handling workflows.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: This site focuses on infrastructure and operational guidance. Genomics analysis pipelines are documented separately.

docs.title: Data Management
docs.text: This section defines how we organize, store, transfer, and protect lab data.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
• Shared directory layout and naming conventions
  • Active vs archival storage
  • Data transfer tools (rsync, rclone, sshfs, scp)
  • GDPR and human genomic data handling
  • Operational best practices

Use the navigation items under Data management for detailed guidance.

docs.title: Dry Lab
docs.text: Dry-lab documentation covers infrastructure usage and reproducible compute workflows.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
• Linux and shell basics
  • Spider and Slurm job execution
  • Conda/Mamba environments
  • Containers (Apptainer/Singularity)
  • Session management with tmux
  • Git and GitHub workflow

Use the navigation items under Dry lab for detailed pages.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
• Spider: SURF HPC platform used by the lab.
  • Slurm: workload manager and job scheduler used on Spider.
  • Login node: entry node for light commands, editing, and job submission.
  • Compute node: node where actual compute jobs run.
  • Batch job: non-interactive job submitted with sbatch.
  • Interactive job: shell allocated through Slurm, usually with srun --pty.
  • Conda/Mamba environment: isolated software environment per project.
  • Apptainer/Singularity: container runtime for reproducible execution on HPC.
  • Symlink: filesystem pointer to another path, created with ln -s.
  • MaxRSS: peak memory usage reported by Slurm accounting tools.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text: This page is the shortest path to getting productive on lab infrastructure.

docs.title: 1. Access Spider (SURF)
docs.text:
Spider documentation: https://doc.spider.surfsara.nl/en/latest/Pages/getting_started.html

Login command:

ssh holstegelab-username@spider.surfsara.nl

docs.title: 2. Understand the execution model
docs.text:
• Use login nodes for light operations only.
  • Submit compute-heavy work through Slurm.
  • Keep long-running interactive work in tmux and/or a Slurm interactive session.

docs.title: 3. First commands after login
docs.text:
pwd
ls -lh
sinfo
squeue -u $USER

docs.title: 4. Non-negotiable rules
docs.text:
• Never run heavy jobs on a login node.
  • Never store human raw genomic data on personal laptops.
  • Never use consumer cloud storage for controlled data.
  • Keep project directories structured and reproducible.

docs.title: 5. Next pages
docs.text:
• Dry lab -> Spider and Slurm
  • Dry lab -> Linux and Shell Basics
  • Data management -> Project Structure

docs.title: Wet Lab
docs.text:
This section is a placeholder for wet-lab documentation.

Planned topics:

  • Sample intake and labeling
  • Metadata checklist before sequencing
  • Handover format between wet and dry lab
  • Quality checkpoints and escalation paths

docs.title: GDPR and Lab Practices
docs.text: We work with human genomic data. Handle all data as controlled data unless explicitly stated otherwise.

docs.title: Mandatory rules
docs.text:
• No raw sequencing data on personal laptops
  • No Dropbox or Google Drive for controlled data
  • No sequencing data by email
  • Use approved SURF infrastructure
  • Follow university GDPR training requirements

If unsure, ask the PI or a senior lab member before transferring or processing data.

docs.title: Operational good practices
docs.text:
Do:

  • Use structured directories
  • Use version control for code and metadata
  • Save environment definitions
  • Log software versions
  • Use Slurm correctly

Avoid:

  • Running heavy jobs on login nodes
  • Installing tools globally in shared contexts
  • Copying large reference genomes per project
  • Inconsistent or ad hoc naming conventions

docs.title: Project Structure and Naming
docs.text:
Main shared location:

/project/holstegelab/Share/

Recommended structure:

projects/
    PROJECT_NAME/
        raw_data/
        processed/
        results/
        logs/
references/
containers/
envs/

Naming recommendations:

  • No spaces in file names
  • Use lowercase and underscores
  • Use dates as YYYYMMDD
  • Avoid ambiguous names such as final_v2_new_fixed

docs.title: Active storage
docs.text: /project/holstegelab/Share/

docs.title: Archive or tape
docs.text:
Use for long-term storage and retrieval, not active analysis workflows.

Reference: https://servicedesk.surf.nl/wiki/spaces/WIKI/pages/29557422/dCache

docs.title: ResearchDrive
docs.text:
Use for collaboration and small dataset sharing.

Reference: https://researchdrive.surf.nl/apps/files/files

docs.title: rsync (recommended)
docs.text: rsync -avh source destination

docs.title: rclone
docs.text: Reference: https://rclone.org/

docs.title: sshfs
docs.text: Mount remote systems locally for interactive file access.

docs.title: scp
docs.text: Copy individual files across hosts.

docs.title: Environments and Containers
docs.text: Use isolated runtime environments for reproducibility.

docs.title: Conda and Mamba
docs.text:
Documentation: - https://docs.conda.io/ - https://mamba.readthedocs.io/

mamba is a faster implementation of conda dependency resolution.

Create environment:

mamba create -n myenv python=3.11

Activate:

conda activate myenv

Export full environment:

conda env export > environment.yml

Export only explicitly installed tools:

conda env --from-history export > environment.yml

Recreate:

mamba env create -f environment.yml

Best practices:

  • One environment per project
  • Store environment.yml in the project repository
  • Do not install tooling into base

docs.title: Apptainer and Singularity
docs.text:
Documentation: https://apptainer.org/docs/

Run container:

apptainer exec container.sif tool --help

Older command:

singularity exec container.sif tool --help

Bind project directory:

apptainer exec --bind /project container.sif tool

Store shared containers in:

/project/holstegelab/Share/containers/

docs.title: Git and GitHub
docs.text:
Basic workflow:

git clone repo
git add file
git commit -m "message"
git push

Rules:

  • Always use .gitignore.
  • Do not commit raw sequencing data.
  • Do not commit large binary artifacts.

Ask for review or help when changing shared project history or repo structure.

docs.title: Linux and Shell Basics
docs.text: After login, you are working in a UNIX terminal.

docs.title: Essential commands
docs.text:
pwd        # show current directory
ls -lh     # list files with human-readable size
cd dir     # change directory
cd ..      # go one level up
mkdir dir  # create directory
rm file    # remove file
cp a b     # copy
mv a b     # move or rename

Manual pages:

man command

docs.title: Absolute path
docs.text:
Starts from /:

/project/holstegelab/Share/my_project/

docs.title: Relative path
docs.text:
Relative to your current directory:

../data/

Use absolute paths in scripts to avoid ambiguity.

docs.title: Symlinks
docs.text:
Symlinks are pointers to files or directories and do not copy data.

Create:

ln -s /real/path/to/file link_name

Example:

ln -s /project/holstegelab/Share/references/hg38.fa hg38.fa

Inspect target:

ls -l

Remove symlink only:

rm link_name

Use symlinks to avoid duplicating large reference files.

docs.title: Spider overview
docs.text:
Spider documentation: https://doc.spider.surfsara.nl/en/latest/Pages/getting_started.html

Login:

ssh holstegelab-username@spider.surfsara.nl

Spider uses Slurm scheduler, shared project storage, and compute nodes. Do not run heavy jobs on login nodes.

docs.title: Slurm references
docs.text:
• General docs: https://slurm.schedmd.com/documentation.html
  • Spider-specific docs: https://doc.spider.surfsara.nl/en/latest/Pages/slurm.html

docs.title: Check available resources
docs.text:
sinfo
squeue -u $USER

docs.title: Interactive job for testing
docs.text: srun --pty --cpus-per-task=4 --mem=16G --time=02:00:00 bash

docs.title: Batch job example
docs.text:
Create job.sh:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=test
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=32G
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#SBATCH --output=logs/%x_%j.out

source ~/.bashrc
conda activate myenv
python script.py

Submit:

sbatch job.sh

Check memory usage after completion:

sacct -j JOBID --format=JobID,MaxRSS,Elapsed

docs.title: tmux Session Management
docs.text:
Use tmux to keep long-running shell sessions alive across disconnects.

Start a new session:

tmux

Detach from session:

Ctrl+b then d

Reattach:

tmux attach

Quick cheatsheet: https://www.themoderncoder.com/uploads/simple-tmux-cheatsheet.jpg

Rule: Never run long commands without tmux or a Slurm job allocation.
